channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Angsty in Ankara
item.description: Can NATO move past a need to keep Trump happy?

item.title: Is Sports Diplomacy Still Possible?
item.description: Hard power has left its mark on this year’s World Cup.

item.title: Russia’s 11-Hour Assault on Kyiv
item.description: The deadly missile and drone bombardment was retaliation for recent Ukrainian attacks on critical Russian infrastructure.

item.title: Gen Z Goes to Hollywood
item.description: Three summer movies reflect a generation’s sensibilities.

item.title: Despite Chinese Mediation Efforts, Taliban-Pakistan Conflict Continues With New Strikes
item.description: The rupture between Afghanistan’s Taliban and their erstwhile allies in Pakistan continues to cause suffering on both sides of the border.

item.title: A Short Economic History of the United States
item.description: From a slave economy to a global empire in 250 years.

item.title: Czech-Taiwan Ties Are Cooling in Rhetoric, Not Reality
item.description: Czechia’s new government has changed its tone on Taiwan, but left most of the substance of the relationship intact.

item.title: America, the Once Global Nation
item.description: At 250, the country is struggling to come to terms with the world it created.

item.title: The MOU Is Paying Off Early for Iran
item.description: Washington and Tehran are grappling over a “memorandum of misunderstanding.”

item.title: Who was behind those drone incursions over Europe?
item.description: A new report finds it was probably Russia

item.title: Asia Is Racing to the Arctic, But It’s Not Easy
item.description: The political momentum for the Northern Sea Route is real. However, the actual data looks a lot less rosy.

item.title: What the ‘Mother of All Trade Deals’ Learned From the India-ASEAN FTA
item.description: India has spent the last 16 years paying for its trade deal with ASEAN. The India-EU FTA would not repeat those mistakes.

item.title: The U.S. at 250: Terminally Ill or Just Very Sick?
item.description: Experts question whether one of the world’s oldest democracies can survive.

item.title: Between Independence and Freedom
item.description: How a fundamental tension in the declaration echoed through U.S. foreign policy.

item.title: Turkey and Israel should trade energy, not insults
item.description: Both have much to gain from being less belligerent

item.title: NATO ponders how to defend Eastern Europe as America pulls back
item.description: German tanks are returning to a region they once razed

item.title: Donald Trump’s reflecting-pool debacle is a gift to America
item.description: What a fitting way to honour its birthday

item.title: Is The Economist always wrong?
item.description: We used artificial intelligence to test the accuracy of our forecasts

item.title: Business
Cartoon: A toast to America’s 250th birthday
item.description: A lighter take on the news

item.title: Politics
Happy birthday America, the ally Europe admires—questioningly
item.description: A tale of two continents

item.title: Volodymyr Zelensky’s surge is a bid to force peace talks
item.description: Ukrainian claims to be winning the drone war are partly propaganda and partly real

item.title: Indonesia gives its best-known entrepreneur a decade in jail
item.description: The technology giant he built is being yanked around by the state

item.title: The Toddyssey
item.description: You could read Homer. You could see the film. Or you could just go to the office

item.title: The next great Middle East rivalry
item.description: Turkey and Israel are natural allies and yet they are increasingly at odds

item.title: Africa’s new middle class is putting down roots in the suburbs
item.description: For a glimpse of the continent’s future, visit the edges of its burgeoning cities

item.title: Uganda’s volatile army chief goes after the press
item.description: Muhoozi Kainerugaba, the president’s son, looks increasingly like a despot-in-waiting

item.title: Africans are turning to Starlink
item.description: They are fed up with the continent’s terrible internet

item.title: Brazilians are going gaga for Chinese brands
item.description: They think China has better tech than America

item.title: The Caribbean has a problem with pesticides
item.description: It uses them more intensively than anywhere in the world

item.title: Promised reforms could change Cuba radically
item.description: But only if they actually go ahead

item.title: Guam: Where America’s 250th Birthday Will First Be Honored
item.description: As the country celebrates its past, let it also recognize the place where its future security begins: an island that has given more to the United States than most Americans will ever know.

item.title: Italy’s Indo-Pacific Strategy Turns Toward Defense Contracts
item.description: Italy is making an unexpectedly assertive return to Southeast Asia – not through trade or development aid, but through defense diplomacy.

item.title: BIMSTEC, Northeast India, and an Overlooked Pillar of the Act East Policy
item.description: India’s Act East Policy should be judged by its ability to change from articulating strategy to putting that strategy into practice in the Northeast, via BIMSETC.

item.title: Forging China-US ‘Constructive Strategic Stability’ Starts in the Nuclear Realm
item.description: Real strategic stability starts with recognizing mutual vulnerability.

item.title: Taiwan’s Drone Industry Take Centerstage in Defense Spending Debate
item.description: Funding for drones became much more hotly discussed in the wake of KMT chair Cheng Li-wun’s trip to the United States last month.

item.title: Where Do Japan and Vietnam Stand?
item.description: A look at the bilateral relationship, and its future direction.

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: Why 2008 was a turning point in America
item.description: Jon Fasman, our senior culture correspondent, explains how the year laid the groundwork for today’s divisions

item.title: Why Corruption Persists in Uzbekistan’s Water Sector
item.description: Real reform in the water sector means building a system that makes corruption harder to commit, easier to detect, and less rewarding for those who might try.

item.title: Reckoning With the Evolving China-Russia Threat
item.description: Don't underestimate the China-Russia partnership; Beijing and Moscow have already cooperated in ways that seemed unthinkable not long ago.

item.title: The US as the World’s Robber Baron
item.description: Like the Gilded Age robber barons, the United States under President Donald Trump is using trade policy to benefit itself at the expense of all other countries. But now that administration surrogates are openly acknowledging the strategy, all other major trading powers will surely respond accordingly.

item.title: Venezuela’s earthquakes are partly America’s problem
item.description: The government’s response has been dire. Its patron has a duty to help

item.title: Venezuelans are furious with the American-backed regime
item.description: But the earthquakes could give it an excuse to keep putting off elections

item.title: The Hypocrisy That Shields Erdogan From Global Criticism
item.description: Rising human rights violations in Turkey should be called out.

item.title: Mahrang Baloch’s Conviction is a Pyrrhic Victory for the Pakistani State
item.description: They may have locked up a popular and powerful activist. But this will only convince more Baloch that there is no space for political protest or dissent.

item.title: The Strategy Behind the Battle for Crimea
item.description: Ukraine’s new offensive on the territory has military stakes far beyond payback.

item.title: India’s government overhauls a vast workfare programme
item.description: Critics say it has been gutted

item.title: Pakistan’s army chief battles with its imprisoned ex-prime minister
item.description: Which of Asim Munir or Imran Khan will prevail?

item.title: America should not imprison frontier AI
item.description: Fable is free. But the technology desperately needs better regulations

item.title: Is Germany’s government finally getting its act together?
item.description: A new set of reforms has lifted sagging spirits in Berlin

item.title: How much will a 4th of July cookout cost this year?
item.description: We calculate the rising price of Americans’ summer staples

item.title: Are stablecoins money?
item.description: Policymakers’ job is to make them safe as well as useful

item.title: Unpacking Venezuela’s peculiar debt restructuring
item.description: Hazy numbers and the absence of the IMF make for an unusual negotiation

item.title: America is anxious, and awesomely powerful
item.description: Restlessness is what prevents the republic from sinking into stagnation

item.title: We woz wrong about oil
item.description: The market bested us. But for The Economist, there is no shame in that

item.title: A Democratic Opening for Azerbaijan
item.description: With Armenia's recent elections likely to usher in an era of greater stability—and less Russian influence—in the South Caucasus, neighboring Azerbaijan is well-positioned to deepen cooperation with the US and Europe. But without appropriate conditions, new strategic partnerships with the West could fail to deliver for Azerbaijanis.

item.title: Singaporean Police Seize $42M Bungalow in Nvidia Chip Smuggling Probe
item.description: Investigators allege that four individuals deliberately concealed the actual end-user of servers thought to contain advanced Nvidia AI chips.

item.title: The End of Hamas
item.description: The convenient fiction of continued menace.

item.title: Why Trump’s Congo Deal Is Falling Apart
item.description: The limits of "America first" in Africa.

item.title: Mr. Prabowo Versus the Market
item.description: The Indonesian state has a long history of inserting itself into the economy in ways that have made investors uneasy.

item.title: How Has the World Changed Since the 2016 South China Sea Ruling?
item.description: The growing global instability and uncertainty will likely influence the future trajectory of the South China Sea disputes.

item.title: A Rare Case of Matricide Shocked Indonesia — and Now the Verdict Is In
item.description: AL, the perpetrator of the crime, was just 12 years of age at the time of her mother’s murder.

item.title: Myanmar Denies ASEAN Chair’s Request for Meeting With Aung San Suu Kyi
item.description: In late May, the military announced that it had transferred the 81-year-old former leader from prison to house arrest.

item.title: Nvidia’s Silent AI Colonialism Is Trapping East Asia in a Fossil-Fueled Hell
item.description: Every new generation of microchips uses more energy, water, and toxic chemicals than the last – with Taiwan and South Korea bearing over 90 percent of the burden.

item.title: Scientists have built a cell in a lab from the ground up
item.description: What do you call an organism with no evolutionary ancestors? SpudCell, apparently

item.title: Scientists take another step towards lab-made life
item.description: What do you call an organism with no evolutionary ancestors? SpudCell, apparently

item.title: What Hormuz and Chinese Sources Reveal About Beijing’s Energy Strategy
item.description: China’s EV push, the Power of Siberia 2 saga, and Chinese imports of raw uranium supplies may appear to be separate issues. In reality, they tell the same story.

item.title: China hushes up a plane crash in the heart of its capital
item.description: A light aircraft flew into Beijing’s tallest building. A news blackout followed
